title: COVID-19 lockdown and gross domestic product for March and June 2020 quarters | Stats NZ
content:
## COVID-19 lockdown and gross domestic product for March and June 2020 quarters
11 June 2020, 10:45am
This page summarises the data source, methodological, and conceptual challenges to the compilation of quarterly gross domestic product (GDP) for the March and June 2020 quarters due to COVID-19.
GDP provides a quarterly snapshot of the performance of the economy and is New Zealand’s official measure of economic growth.
Gross domestic product: March 2020 quarter , due to be published on 18 June 2020, covers the period of travel restrictions that culminated in the closure of New Zealand’s borders on 19 March 2020. It also covers the start of the alert level 4 lockdown period (from midnight 25 March), which represented six days or about 7 percent of the quarter.
The full impact of the lockdown will be observed in Gross domestic product: June 2020 quarter (due to be published on 17 September 2020). We will publish a further update ahead of the June 2020 quarter release and will include supplementary material in upcoming GDP releases. This ensures we are transparent about data quality and are clear about how we adjusted our approach to respond to the impacts of COVID-19 on our statistics.
The scope of the impacts is wide and poses operational and measurement challenges on many fronts. Stats NZ is not alone in facing these challenges and is engaged with the international statistical community to respond in a way that is consistent with international best practice as far as possible. Many impacts are specific to New Zealand’s context, but there is commonality in many of the general issues.
- Methodological challenges
- Approach to dealing with methodological challenges in March 2020 quarter
The move to COVID-19 alert level 4 affected data collection by Stats NZ, including input data that are used to compile Gross Domestic Product: March 2020 quarter . Alternative approaches and means of contacting businesses were implemented.  Input data sources used in Gross Domestic Product: March 2020 quarter saw response and imputation rates close to those typically achieved.
The key areas where data quality has been affected in March 2020 quarter were:
- International Visitors Survey (IVS) – border restrictions resulted in a lower response rate and has affected the reliability of the IVS estimates. IVS is used to estimate travel expenditure by visitors. In quarterly GDP it contributes to exports of travel services, and breakdowns by purpose of travel are used in compiling household consumption expenditure. Impacts of COVID-19 on Goods and services trade by country: Year ended March 2020 has further information.
- Value of building work put into place: March 2020 quarter saw impacts to direct collection and modelling used to estimate low- to medium value projects. These estimates have been published with a provisional status and are subject to revision. Value of building work put into place data are used in estimating construction output in GDP production, and the residential and non-residential building components of gross fixed capital formation in GDP expenditure. COVID-19 lockdown and value of building work put into place for March 2020 quarter has further information.

### Scope and coverage of data sources
The scope, coverage and methodologies underpinning some of our input data sources mean that these input data sources may have difficulty measuring COVID-19 related effects. The main instances of this are:
- The Quarterly Employment Survey (QES) is a mid-quarter estimate. The March 2020 quarter QES release reflected conditions in the pay weeks ended, or immediately before, 20 February 2020. The June 2020 quarter QES release will reflect conditions in the pay weeks ending on, or immediately before, 20 May, effectively missing the alert level 4 period. Employment indicators from the QES are used to measure components of both GDP production and GDP expenditure. We are reviewing all QES-based indicators used in GDP production and expenditure and confronting them against other data sources. This will enable us to make informed adjustments to QES-based indicators in cases where they do not represent disrupted activity resulting from COVID-19.
- The value of building work put into place is estimated using a model-based approach for low- to medium value projects based on historical data. The behavior of building projects is likely to have changed with the disruption caused by COVID-19. The model is currently being reviewed and additional information is being collected to support capturing COVID-19 impacts.
We will continue to monitor the following areas over the next few quarters:
- collection of data from businesses that have ceased part way through the quarter/period
- any changes to the timing or coverage of administrative data sources
- data that could not be collected during the lockdown period, and how missing data is dealt with (for example, imputation methods or alternative data sources)
- availability of new data sources that we can use for analysis, confrontation and/or incorporation in the quarterly GDP estimates.

## Methodological challenges
The quarterly indicators used to compile our estimate of GDP are the best that we have available at that point in time. When compiling our quarterly estimates, we face tradeoffs between timeliness and the quality, coverage, length of historical series and/or conceptual alignment of available data sources. As a result, the methodologies that we use vary depending on the data available and are underpinned by certain assumptions. In normal periods of activity, these assumptions provide us with reasonable estimates of activity. In periods of abrupt disruption, resulting in changes to activity and temporary shifts in the structure of the economy, some of these relationships may weaken or no longer hold.
All methods used in quarterly GDP are summarized in Quarterly gross domestic product: Sources and methods (fifth edition).
The main challenges that COVID-19 and its impacts on activity pose for our usual methodologies  are outlined below.

### 1. Using indicators that assume a fixed relationship
Volume extrapolation (a volume index is used to reflect the change in quantity over time) of value added (which is equal to gross output minus intermediate consumption) assumes that gross output, intermediate consumption, and therefore value-added, move in a similar fashion, with the movement proxied by the quarterly indicator chosen. The indicators used in quarterly GDP are output and factor of production, which are detailed below.
Output indicators, such as sale volumes or passenger numbers, assume that the change in output is a good proxy for the change in value added. These indicators will not be able to capture any changes in the relationships between intermediate consumption, output, and value added that may emerge due to a significant economic shock. For example, the indicator for components of the retail and accommodation industries is the volume of sales. During the lockdown, sales in parts of these industries  have fallen sharply due to the closure of shops and restriction of non-essential activity. Our method assumes that the ratio between output and intermediate consumption is stable. However, intermediate consumption could have fallen by less than the fall in sales volumes, due to fixed costs such as rent still being incurred. Additional costs may have also been incurred, for example retail businesses needing to change their operations in light of new health and safety requirements, or restaurants and cafes adapting by changing the services they provide. Our current quarterly measure will overestimate value added if this impact is significant.
Volume extrapolation using output indicators is the method for most sub-components of GDP production.  Note that in most cases, despite its limitations, it remains the best method on a quarterly basis due to data availability.
The annual national accounts, balanced through the supply-use framework, are what we rely on to understand and reflect changes in the structure of the economy and relationships between different variables such as gross output, intermediate consumption, and value added.  The full effect of restrictions due to COVID-19 and the alert level 4 lockdown in the first half of 2020 will be incorporated in Gross domestic product: September 2022 quarter (due in December 2022), which is when balanced annual data for the year ended March 2021 will be available.
The higher degree of uncertainty around our quarterly estimates due to the abrupt shift in economic activity and limitations of our quarterly indicators may affect the degree of coherence between our quarterly GDP production and GDP expenditure measures. It is also likely to entail larger revisions when we incorporate the annual data.
We have and will continue to review our current data sources and methods for all industries and investigate data from alternate sources such as the Business Data Collection . We may introduce new methods and data sources where appropriate and will be transparent about any changes made.

#### Factor of production indicators
Factor of production indicators include the use of employment indicators such as hours paid, employee counts, and wages and salaries to measure activity, primarily to measure activity in the service industries. They are used in both GDP production and GDP expenditure. While employment indicators are a reasonable proxy for economic activity in usual periods, the introduction of the wage subsidy schemes affects the suitability of these indicators for some industries. The wage subsidy scheme supports employees working full, part, or no hours while still being paid. In this case our quarterly indicators will overestimate activity during the lockdown period. Conversely, there may be some sub-industries where salaried employees are working longer hours – however, salaries paid do not reflect changes in hours worked. In this case we would underestimate activity.
This is an issue for measuring business services, arts and recreation, and market output for the health sector. We will confront all components that use employment indicators against alternative data sources such as GST sales, Monthly Employment Indicators and Business Data Collection to understand activity during the period. These data sources will either inform adjustments to the employment indicators used, or where appropriate may allow us to implement new methodologies that better capture activity.

### 2. Methods without quarterly indicators
Some sub-components of GDP production and GDP expenditure do not use quarterly indicators, and instead derive quarterly movements between annual estimates (interpolation) or project forward from an annual benchmark (extrapolation). Such indicators are used where little short-term variation is expected and will fail to capture sudden changes in economic activity.
In most cases, this method is used for small sub-components which do not have material impacts on higher level aggregates. One exception is the education industry, where all sub-industry components lack a quarterly indicator. As a result, using the existing method will fail to capture the fall in international students due to travel restrictions and border closures, and the impacts of the level 4 lockdown on activity in the sector.
We are currently reviewing all components that do not have quarterly indicators. Where possible we will source and use alternative data sources to make adjustments, and in future quarters may implement new methods. For example, for the March 2020 quarter estimates of education we have sourced student numbers to adjust for the effect of falling international student numbers on tertiary education output, and we have also made adjustments to account for disrupted output in the sector resulting from the alert level 4 lockdown.

## Approach to dealing with methodological challenges in March 2020 quarter
Analysis and confrontation of quarterly GDP estimates using a range of quantitative and qualitative data sources is a standard part of our quality assurance process. As part of this process we may make informed adjustments to quarterly indicators that do not adequately represent activity.
For the March 2020 quarter we have used data sources such as the Business Data Collection, GST sales, and Monthly Employment Indicators to assist in understanding the level of activity across the components of GDP production and GDP expenditure and, where known limitations in our quarterly indicators exist, allow us to make informed adjustments.
We have not made any permanent changes to our methodologies or input data sources in our March 2020 quarter release. We will continue to assess our methodologies and available data and may introduce changes in future quarters if appropriate to do so.
Further information on where we have made adjustments will be included in Gross domestic product: March 2020 quarter.
Sharp changes in activity, such as those caused by the lockdown, pose challenges for our usual seasonal adjustment processes. This has affected some series in the March 2020 quarter and will be a widespread issue in the June 2020 quarter. We have reviewed all series exhibiting unusual changes in activity and have applied outliering of the latest data point where required. This has the effect of subduing the impact of unusual data points on the seasonal adjustment process. We will continue to monitor our seasonal adjustment over the next few quarters and will make interventions where required.
The New Zealand Government announced a range of support measures in response to the impacts of COVID-19. It is important that we classify these transactions through the National Accounts. Some measures, such as the wage subsidy scheme, are similar to schemes introduced in other countries, and international guidance has been released to inform the treatment of these and other government responses to COVID-19.
The table below summarises the elements of New Zealand’s support packages that we have classified so far. We will provide further information as more elements of the support packages are assessed. Please note that the packages below will not be directly measured in quarterly GDP production or GDP expenditure. They will be captured in National Accounts – (Income and Expenditure) and Government Financial Statistics, which are released annually.

### Classification of government support packages in the National Accounts
